sentences.grammarNote: Resultative complements: verb + result. **起晚** = 'woke up late' (**起** = get up, **晚** = late). Other examples: 写错 (wrote wrong), 听懂 (listened and understood), 说清楚 (explained clearly). The result directly follows the verb.

**错过** (cuòguò) = 'to miss (an opportunity or vehicle)'. **错** = wrong/mistakenly, **过** = to pass. Together: to mistakenly let something pass by.
The **了** after the verb marks a completed (unfortunate) event. **公交车** (gōngjiāochē) = public bus (**公交** = public transit, **车** = vehicle).

**迟到** (chídào) = to be late / arrive late. **迟** = late/delayed, **到** = to arrive.
**上班** (shàngbān) = to go to work / be at work (**上** = up/onto, **班** = shift/class). Compare **下班** (xiàbān) = to get off work.
The **了** at the end marks a change of situation — 'being late' has now become a fact.

sentences.romanized: Wǒ de lǎobǎn hěn bù gāoxìng.
sentences.grammarNote:
**不高兴** (bù gāoxìng) = 'unhappy'. Chinese often forms negatives by adding **不** to a positive adjective: **高兴**→**不高兴**, **开心**→**不开心**. This is more natural than a single word like 'unhappy' in many cases.
**高兴** = **高** (high) + **兴** (spirit/interest) → high spirits → happy.
**很不高兴** = very unhappy — **很** modifies the whole phrase **不高兴**.

**感到** (gǎndào) = 'to feel / sense'. **有压力** = 'have pressure/stress'. Compare: 我很紧张 (I'm nervous) — using **有** + noun to describe a state is very common in Chinese.
**压力** = **压** (to press) + **力** (force/strength) → pressure/stress.

**休息** (xiūxi) = to rest / take a break. **休** = to rest, **息** = breath/rest — both components relate to resting.
**一下** (yīxià) after a verb softens the tone: 'rest a bit / take a little break.' It also suggests the action is brief and casual.
**需要** (xūyào) = to need (followed directly by a verb or noun).

sentences.base_language: I'm going for a short walk to clear my head.
sentences.romanized: Wǒ qù sàn sàn bù, qīngxǐng yīxià.
sentences.grammarNote:
**散散步** is verb reduplication: **散步** (take a walk) → **散散步** (take a little walk). Reduplicating a verb suggests doing the action briefly and casually. It also softens the tone.
**清醒** (qīngxǐng) = clear-headed / to become alert. **清** = clear, **醒** = awake/alert.
**清醒一下** = clear one's head a bit — another use of **一下** to soften and suggest brevity.

sentences.base_language: Tomorrow will be a better day.
sentences.grammarNote:
**会** (huì) expresses prediction or expectation: 'will / is expected to'. **更** (gèng) = 'even more / more': **更好** (better, literally 'more good'). The sentence-final **的** in this pattern softens the statement into a confident assertion.
This is an encouraging pattern to know: **会更好的** = 'it will get better' — very commonly used to comfort someone.
